content.text: vLLM is an open-source LLM inference engine built around two ideas: PagedAttention for near-zero KV-cache memory waste, and continuous batching at the iteration level to keep GPU utilisation high. It exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST API so existing code that calls
can point at a local vLLM server with a one-line URL change.

attrs.alt: vLLM architecture: Scheduler, BlockSpaceManager, KV-cache, and GPU ModelExecutor

content.text: The three core components work together on every decode step:
— maintains waiting, running, and swapped sequence queues. On each step it calls
object describing which sequences to run, which KV-cache blocks to swap in from CPU, which to swap out, and which to copy (for parallel sampling / beam search).
— manages two pools of fixed-size KV-cache pages (GPU and CPU). Each sequence gets a
mapping logical token positions to physical block IDs. Blocks are reference-counted so sequences that share a common prefix share those physical blocks without copying.
— runs the PagedAttention CUDA kernel that reads from non-contiguous GPU memory using the block tables, then calls the sampler to pick the next token per sequence.
Attention requires access to all past KV pairs for a sequence. Naive implementations allocate a contiguous buffer of size

per sequence at creation time — most of which is wasted because output length is unknown. PagedAttention instead divides KV-cache into fixed-size blocks (typically 16 tokens) and allocates them on demand, one block per 16 new tokens generated.

The CUDA kernel for PagedAttention receives the block table and gathers KV data from scattered physical addresses. There is no memory copy — the kernel dereferences the block table at attention time. This cuts KV-cache waste to less than one block per sequence and allows the engine to run 4–10× more concurrent sequences on the same GPU compared to contiguous allocation.

# Install (requires Python 3.9+, CUDA 11.8+)
pip install vllm

# Serve Llama-3-8B on localhost:8000 with the OpenAI-compatible API
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
    --tensor-parallel-size 1 \
    --max-model-len 8192 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
    --port 8000

# Test with curl
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"model": "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
          "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
          "max_tokens": 64}'
Number of GPUs to shard the model across (tensor parallelism)
Number of pipeline stages across GPUs (pipeline parallelism)
Fraction of GPU HBM reserved for KV-cache after model weights
Max total tokens (prompt + output) per sequence
Max concurrent sequences in a single iteration
Reuse KV blocks for identical prefixes across requests
Calling vLLM with the OpenAI SDK

from openai import OpenAI

# Point the SDK at your vLLM server — no other code changes needed
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-required",   # vLLM ignores this by default
)

# Chat completion
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a concise assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is PagedAttention?"},
    ],
    max_tokens=256,
    temperature=0.7,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Streaming
stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Count to 5"}],
    max_tokens=64,
    stream=True,
)
for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
    if delta:
        print(delta, end="", flush=True)
print()
Using the vLLM Python API Directly
For offline batch inference or when you don't need an HTTP server, use the synchronous LLM class:

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    tensor_parallel_size=1,
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.85,
)

params = SamplingParams(
    temperature=0.8,
    top_p=0.95,
    max_tokens=256,
)

prompts = [
    "Explain gradient descent in one paragraph.",
    "What is the capital of France?",
    "Write a Python function to reverse a string.",
]

outputs = llm.generate(prompts, params)
for output in outputs:
    print(f"Prompt: {output.prompt!r}")
    print(f"Output: {output.outputs[0].text!r}")
    print()
with an asyncio interface for use inside async web frameworks like FastAPI:

import asyncio
from vllm.engine.async_llm_engine import AsyncLLMEngine
from vllm.engine.arg_utils import AsyncEngineArgs
from vllm.sampling_params import SamplingParams
from vllm.utils import random_uuid

async def main():
    engine_args = AsyncEngineArgs(
        model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
        tensor_parallel_size=1,
        gpu_memory_utilization=0.85,
    )
    engine = AsyncLLMEngine.from_engine_args(engine_args)

    request_id = random_uuid()
    params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.7, max_tokens=200)

    # generate() is an async generator — yields RequestOutput on each token
    async for output in engine.generate(
        prompt="Explain vLLM in three sentences.",
        sampling_params=params,
        request_id=request_id,
    ):
        # output.outputs[0].text grows with each token
        if output.finished:
            print(output.outputs[0].text)

asyncio.run(main())
When many requests share a common prefix (system prompt, few-shot examples, a document), prefix caching reuses the KV blocks for those tokens rather than recomputing them. Enable it with
. The cache is keyed by a hash of the token IDs in each block — if an incoming request's prefix matches cached blocks, those blocks are attached to the new sequence's block table at zero cost.

# With prefix caching enabled, repeated system prompts are nearly free
# The first request computes and caches the system prompt KV blocks
# Subsequent requests reuse them

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1", api_key="x")

SYSTEM = """You are an expert Python tutor. Always show complete, runnable examples.
Explain each line. Use PEP 8 style. Mention potential pitfalls."""

# First call: system prompt is computed and cached
r1 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain list comprehensions"},
    ],
    max_tokens=300,
)

# Second call: system prompt KV blocks are reused — TTFB is lower
r2 = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain generators"},
    ],
    max_tokens=300,
)
vLLM supports two parallelism strategies for models too large for one GPU:
Each attention head / FFN column split across N GPUs; all-reduce after each layer
Models that fit in N×GPU VRAM; same node
Model layers split into stages; each GPU holds consecutive layers
Models that need more VRAM than tensor-parallel can provide; multi-node
Lower — point-to-point between stages; adds bubble overhead

# 4×GPU tensor parallelism — all 4 GPUs process every layer in parallel
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct \
    --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
    --gpu-memory-utilization 0.92

# 2-node setup: TP=4 within each node, PP=2 across nodes (8 GPUs total)
# Node 0 (ranks 0-3): layers 0-39
# Node 1 (ranks 4-7): layers 40-79
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct \
    --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
    --pipeline-parallel-size 2 \
    --distributed-executor-backend ray
vLLM integrates several quantisation formats to reduce VRAM usage and increase throughput:

# Serve a pre-quantised AWQ model (4-bit weights, ~2× VRAM saving)
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model TheBloke/Llama-2-13B-chat-AWQ \
    --quantization awq \
    --tensor-parallel-size 1

# FP8 on H100 — highest throughput option
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct \
    --quantization fp8 \
    --tensor-parallel-size 4
Sequences queued, waiting for a slot
Fraction of KV-cache blocks in use

# Scrape metrics
curl -s http://localhost:8000/metrics | grep vllm:

# Example output
# vllm:num_requests_running 12
# vllm:num_requests_waiting 3
# vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc 0.62
# vllm:time_to_first_token_seconds{quantile="0.99"} 0.423
# vllm:time_per_output_token_seconds{quantile="0.99"} 0.038

vLLM's core innovations — PagedAttention (near-zero KV-cache waste via virtual memory paging) and continuous batching (iteration-level scheduling) — together deliver state-of-the-art LLM serving throughput. The OpenAI-compatible API means almost no code change for migration. For production: tune

so the KV-cache is large without triggering OOM, enable prefix caching when serving a fixed system prompt, use tensor parallelism within a node and pipeline parallelism across nodes for 70B+ models, and watch
